- KAN-37: Design tokens should block KAN-38 (controls), which then blocks KAN-39 and KAN-40 (landing+results and wardrobe pass). So the dependency chain is KAN-37 → KAN-38 → KAN-39, KAN-40.

- KAN-39: Restyle Landing + Results screens - depends on KAN-38
- KAN-40: Wardrobe y2k pass - depends on KAN-38

- KAN-42: BE webhook + entitlement - depends on KAN-41, blocks KAN-43

1 # my-harness 2 3 Personal harness for agentic workflows: reusable agents and GitHub Actions 4 glue that I drop into projects. 5 6 ## Contents 7 8 - [What's here](#whats-here) 9 - [Use this harness in another project](#use-this-harness-in-another-project) 10 - [GitHub Actions configuration](#github-actions-configuration) 11 - [Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger)](#jira--github-automation-dev-ready-trigger) 12 - [Adding a new agent](#adding-a-new-agent) 13 14 ## What's here 15 16 - [`agents/pr-review`](agents/pr-review) — comment-only PR reviewer. 17 - [`agents/jira-ticket`](agents/jira-ticket) — daily Jira backlog groomer. 18 - [`agents/junior-dev`](agents/junior-dev) — headless implementer for small 19 tickets (SP ≤ 3). 20 - [`agents/senior-dev`](agents/senior-dev) — OpenSpec-driven implementer 21 for complex tickets, with a human-in-the-loop pause on the spec PR. 22 - [`agents/agent-improver`](agents/agent-improver) — meta-agent that 23 proposes prompt edits from execution logs. 24 - [`.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml`](.github/workflows/orchestrator.yml) 25 — deterministic Jira → junior/senior router on the **Dev Ready** 26 transition. 27 - [`scripts/install-harness.sh`](scripts/install-harness.sh) — copy this harness into 28 another repo (see below). 29 - [`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](docs/agentic-workflow.md) — how the pieces 30 fit together. 31 32 --- 33 34 ## Use this harness in another project 35 36 `scripts/install-harness.sh` `rsync`s every tracked file from this repo into a 37 target repo, skipping per-clone state (`.env`, `.jira/config.yml`, 38 `bin/.jira-bin`, agent-log history). Default is dry-run. 39 40 ```sh 41 # from anywhere 42 bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/install-harness.sh ~/Dev/other-repo # dry-run 43 bash /path/to/my-harness/scripts/install-harness.sh ~/Dev/other-repo --apply # do it 44 ``` 45 46 What gets copied: `.github/workflows/`, `agents/`, `.claude/`, `.mcp.json`, 47 `scripts/`, `tools/`, `docs/`, `openspec/`, `.env.example`, `.jira/.gitkeep`. 48 49 What's left alone in the target: 50 - The target's existing `README.md` — never overwritten. 51 - The target's existing `.gitignore` — harness rules are appended in a 52 delimited `# --- my-harness ---` block. Re-running won't duplicate. 53 - Anything not tracked in the harness (project source, `node_modules`, etc.). 54 55 After bootstrapping, run the per-clone setup in the target repo: 56 57 ```sh 58 # 1. project-local jira binary into ./bin/ (no brew needed) 59 bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh 60 61 # 2. paste tokens into a gitignored .env 62 cp .env.example .env 63 # JIRA_API_TOKEN=... https://id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens 64 # CONTEXT7_API_KEY=... https://context7.com/dashboard 65 66 # 3. wire this clone to a Jira instance / project 67 bash scripts/jira-init.sh 68 ``` 69 70 `jira-init.sh` writes `.jira/config.yml` (gitignored). For day-to-day CLI 71 use: `source .env && export JIRA_CONFIG_FILE=$PWD/.jira/config.yml`, or 72 let the `bin/jira` wrapper handle it (it auto-injects `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` 73 from the repo root). 74 75 --- 76 77 ## GitHub Actions configuration 78 79 The workflows read repo **secrets** (sensitive) and repo **variables** 80 (non-sensitive) from *Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions*. Set 81 these once per target repo. 82 83 ### Secrets 84 85 | Name | Used by | Source | 86 |---|---|---| 87 | `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` | every Claude-driven workflow | Run `claude` locally and grab the token, or use an Anthropic API key | 88 | `JIRA_API_TOKEN` | jira-ticket, orchestrator, junior-dev, senior-dev | | 89 | `CONTEXT7_API_KEY` | workflows that pass it through to the Context7 MCP | | 90 | `SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN` | jira-ticket Slack summary | Slack app *Bot User OAuth Token* (`xoxb-…`) with `chat:write` | 91 | `DEV_PAT` *(optional)* | senior-dev / junior-dev when they need to push to a protected branch | Fine-grained PAT, `Contents: read/write` + `Pull requests: read/write`. Falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` if unset | 92 93 ### Variables 94 95 | Name | Required? | Purpose | 96 |---|---|---| 97 | `JIRA_SERVER` | yes | e.g. `https://yourworkspace.atlassian.net` | 98 | `JIRA_LOGIN` | yes | Atlassian account email | 99 | `JIRA_PROJECT_KEY` | yes | Project the agents groom / route on | 100 | `JIRA_BOARD_ID` | optional | Defaults to `0` | 101 | `SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID` | only if you want jira-ticket Slack summaries | Channel ID (not name) — `C0123456789` | 102 | `AGENT_APP_BOT_LOGIN` | yes (if running pr-review on App-authored PRs) | The App's bot login, e.g. `narcie-dev[bot]`. Wired into `pr-review.yml`'s `allowed_bots` so `claude-code-action` doesn't reject senior/junior-dev PRs as bot-initiated. | 103 104 ### Things to review per repo 105 106 Every workflow under `.github/workflows/` was written for *this* repo. 107 After bootstrapping, scan the new repo's copy for: 108 109 - **Cron times** in `jira-ticket.yml` (currently `17 7 * * *` UTC). 110 - **Triggers** (`pull_request` types, `pull_request_review` types) in 111 `pr-review.yml` and `senior-dev-resume.yml` — adjust if the project's 112 branching model differs. 113 - **`max_turns` / `timeout-minutes`** on dev-agent workflows — defaults 114 bias toward small tickets. 115 116 --- 117 118 ## Jira → GitHub automation (Dev Ready trigger) 119 120 The orchestrator only runs when Jira tells it a ticket is ready. Wire 121 this **once per Jira project** (the rule lives in Jira automation, not 122 in code). 123 124 ### 1. Create a fine-grained GitHub PAT 125 126 In *github.com → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → 127 Fine-grained tokens*: 128 129 - **Repository access:** only the target repo. 130 - **Permissions:** 131 - `Contents: Read` 132 - `Actions: Read and write` 133 134 Copy the token — you'll paste it into Jira next. 135 136 ### 2. Store the PAT as a Jira automation secret 137 138 In Jira: *Project settings → Automation → … → Manage secrets*. 139 Add a secret, e.g. `GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT`, paste the token, save. 140 Jira automation rules can reference it as `{{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`. 141 142 ### 3. Add the automation rule 143 144 *Project settings → Automation → Create rule*: 145 146 - **Trigger:** *Issue transitioned* → To status: **Dev Ready**. 147 - **(Optional) Conditions:** issue type is *Story* / *Sub-task*; project 148 is the right one; story points field is set (the orchestrator fails 149 loudly on unpointed tickets, so you can pre-filter here). 150 - **Action:** *Send web request*. 151 152 | Field | Value | 153 |---|---| 154 | URL | `https://api.github.com/repos///dispatches` | 155 | Method | `POST` | 156 | Headers | `Authorization: Bearer {{secrets.GITHUB_DISPATCH_PAT}}`
`Accept: application/vnd.github+json`
`Content-Type: application/json` | 157 | Body type | Custom data | 158 | Body | `{"event_type":"jira-dev-ready","client_payload":{"ticket_key":"{{issue.key}}"}}` | 159 160 Tick *Wait for response* if you want failures to surface in the rule's 161 audit log. 162 163 Save and enable the rule. 164 165 ### 4. Verify 166 167 Move a pointed ticket to **Dev Ready**. Within ~30 seconds: 168 169 - *Jira → Project settings → Automation → Audit log* shows the rule 170 firing with HTTP 204 (the GitHub dispatches endpoint returns 204 on 171 success, no body). 172 - *GitHub → Actions → Orchestrator* shows a new run with 173 `repository_dispatch` as the trigger. 174 - The orchestrator either dispatches `junior-dev.yml` / `senior-dev.yml` 175 by story points, or fails the run with a "no story points" error. 176 177 ### 5. Linking PRs back to tickets 178 179 Jira's **Smart Commits** auto-link if the Jira key (e.g. `KAN-42`) 180 appears in: 181 182 - Commit messages 183 - Branch names 184 - PR titles or descriptions 185 186 The dev agents already prefix branches and PR titles with the ticket 187 key, so links appear under *Development* on the ticket once the GitHub 188 ↔ Jira app is connected (*Apps → GitHub for Jira* on the Atlassian 189 side, one-time per workspace). 190 191 --- 192 193 ## Adding a new agent 194 195 1. Drop the prompt at `agents//prompt.md`. 196 2. Wire a workflow at `.github/workflows/.yml` that feeds the 197 prompt to `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` (or `claude -p` for 198 implementers). 199 3. Make sure the secrets/vars it references are listed above; add any 200 new ones to this README. 201 202 203 204 205

1	# Agentic Development Workflow
2	
3	> **Status:** First-draft vision. Extracted from a hand-drawn diagram (2026-06-04).
4	> Not yet scoped, designed, or implemented.
5	
6	The flow describes an end-to-end pipeline where humans create tickets and merge
7	final PRs, and a chain of agents handles everything in between: ticket grooming,
8	complexity routing, implementation, testing, review, and refinement.
9	
10	## High-level flow
11	
12	```
13	Humans create tickets
14	        │
15	        ▼
16	   Ticket Agent
17	        │
18	        ▼
19	Development Orchestrator
20	        │
21	        ├──── complex ────► Senior developer agent  (OpenSpec /opsx)
22	        │                          │
23	        │                          ▼
24	        │                    PR Review agent ◄────┐
25	        │                          ▲              │
26	        │                          ▼              │
27	        │                    PR Refinement agent  │
28	        │                          │              │
29	        │                          ▼              │
30	        │                Humans test & merge PR   │
31	        │                                         │
32	        └──── easy ──────► Junior developer agent │
33	                                  │   (headless `claude -p`, --max-turns)
34	                                  ▼               │
35	                              Draft PR ───────────┘
36	                          (human picks up &
37	                           merges, or re-runs)
38	```
39	
40	## Human touchpoints
41	
42	- **Start:** humans create tickets.
43	- **End:** humans test the final PR and merge it.
44	- **Mid-loop (complex path only):** the Senior developer agent pulls a human
45	  into the loop before implementing.
46	
47	## Agents
48	
49	### Ticket Agent
50	**Goal:** manage Jira tickets.
51	
52	- Does **not** invent new tickets.
53	- Works on a daily basis. 
54	- Decomposes tickets and improves descriptions based on the current code.
55	- Creates dependencies between tickets (`blocks`, `depends on`).
56	
57	### Development Orchestrator
58	**Goal:** decide which development agent to launch for each ticket.
59	
60	- **Deterministic, no LLM.** Pure routing on Jira story points: SP ≤ 3 →
61	  junior, SP ≥ 4 → senior. Unpointed tickets fail the workflow loudly so
62	  the team re-estimates before anything implements.
63	- **Triggered per-ticket** by a Jira automation rule on the "Dev Ready"
64	  status transition (GitHub `repository_dispatch`), not on a daily cron.
65	
66	### Senior Developer Agent — OpenSpec (`/opsx` conventions)
67	**Goal:** tackle complex tasks **with a human in the loop**.
68	
69	- Uses spec-driven development via the [OpenSpec](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec)
70	  library (the originally sketched `/openspec:explore` doesn't exist;
71	  OpenSpec's `/opsx:*` commands are the real equivalent — see
72	  [`agents/senior-dev/README.md`](../agents/senior-dev/README.md)).
73	- Requests human input before moving to implementation: spec lands as a
74	  Draft PR; a native PR review (Approve / Request changes) resumes the
75	  agent into implement / revise.
76	- Same runtime shape as Junior: headless `claude -p` on a GHA runner with
77	  `--max-turns` and a workflow timeout. The OpenSpec conventions and
78	  human-in-the-loop pauses layer on top of that base.
79	
80	### Junior Developer Agent
81	**Goal:** tackle easy tasks **without a human in the loop**.
82	
83	- Headless `claude -p` invoked directly from a GitHub Actions workflow
84	  (no skill wrapper). Plans straight through to execution.
85	- Bounded by `--max-turns` per dispatch + a workflow-level `timeout-minutes`.
86	- Creates a Draft PR; a human picks it up from there.
87	
88	### PR Review Agent
89	**Goal:** daily review of updated PRs.
90	
91	- Inline comments.
92	- Severity.
93	- Reasoning.
94	
95	### PR Refinement Agent
96	**Goal:** daily refinement of the PR.
97	
98	- May use a different model (e.g. CODEX).
99	- Counteracts the review (acts as a deliberate counter-voice to the reviewer).
100	
101	## Design choices worth calling out
102	
103	- **Complexity gate at the orchestrator.** Two-tier routing keeps the
104	  human-in-the-loop cost only on work that needs it.
105	- **Runtime split.** `claude-code-action` for read/triage surfaces
106	  (ticket grooming, PR review, agent-improver); raw `claude -p` for
107	  implementation lanes (junior-dev, senior-dev); plain bash for the
108	  orchestrator (no LLM). The action's PR-comment plumbing is dead weight
109	  when the agent's job is to edit files, push a branch, and open a PR.
110	- **Bounded dev-agent runs.** `--max-turns` per dispatch + `timeout-minutes`
111	  on the job stop a hard ticket from spinning forever. Tickets stay in
112	  **In Progress** so the orchestrator won't re-fire them.
113	- **Review ↔ Refinement as an adversarial pair.** Using a different model for
114	  refinement is a deliberate hedge against single-model review bias.
115	- **Daily cadence** on review and refinement rather than per-PR-event triggers.
116	
117	## Open questions (for follow-up)
118	
119	- How does the Refinement agent know when to stop counteracting?
120	- How are the two daily passes (review + refinement) sequenced — same run, or
121	  staggered?
122

1	# Senior Developer agent
2	
3	Spec-driven implementation agent for "complex" Jira tickets (story
4	points ≥ 4), with a **human checkpoint between spec and
5	implementation**. The agent explores the code, writes an OpenSpec
6	change proposal, opens a Draft PR, and stops. A human reviews the spec
7	in the PR; their review verdict resumes the agent into revision or
8	implementation. The checkpoint exists to stop wasted implementation
9	work on misunderstood requirements.
10	
11	Part of the agentic workflow sketched in
12	[`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](../../docs/agentic-workflow.md).
13	
14	## Files
15	
16	- `prompt.md` — the agent's prompt (phase-conditional). Edit this to
17	  change behavior.
18	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev.yml` — workflow that runs the
19	  agent (all three phases).
20	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev-resume.yml` — thin
21	  `pull_request_review` trigger that maps human review verdicts to
22	  phase dispatches.
23	- `../../openspec/` — OpenSpec scaffolding (`openspec init`), where
24	  change proposals live.
25	
26	## How it runs
27	
28	```
29	orchestrator ──dispatch(ticket_key)──► senior-dev.yml [phase=propose]
30	                                          │ explore, write OpenSpec change,
31	                                          │ open Draft PR, STOP
32	                                          ▼
33	              human reviews the spec diff in the Draft PR
34	              (comments = discussion; nothing runs meanwhile)
35	                          │                            │
36	              "Request changes" review          "Approve" review
37	                          │                            │
38	              senior-dev-resume.yml (draft-gated, permission-checked)
39	                          │                            │
40	            senior-dev.yml [phase=revise]   senior-dev.yml [phase=implement]
41	              update spec, push, STOP         execute tasks.md, push,
42	              (review again)                  mark PR ready-for-review
43	                                              (pr-review.yml takes over)
44	```
45	
46	There is **no running process while a human reviews** — each phase is
47	a separate headless `claude -p` run, and all state lives on the
48	`senior-dev/<TICKET_KEY>` branch and its PR thread. A resumed run
49	re-reads the ticket, the committed spec, and the full PR conversation
50	(including inline review comments) from scratch.
51	
52	Dispatches come from three places:
53	
54	- **Automated** — the Development Orchestrator fires `phase=propose`
55	  on the Jira "Dev Ready" transition for every ticket with story
56	  points ≥ 4, then moves the ticket to **In Progress**.
57	- **Reviews** — `senior-dev-resume.yml` fires `phase=revise` /
58	  `phase=implement` from human review verdicts (see below).
59	- **Manual** — `gh workflow run senior-dev.yml -f ticket_key=KAN-42
60	  [-f phase=...]`.
61	
62	## Decision: spec exploration via the OpenSpec library
63	
64	KAN-4 required choosing how the spec gets produced, because the
65	`/openspec:explore` command referenced in `docs/agentic-workflow.md`
66	**does not exist** — it is neither a built-in Claude Code command nor
67	a skill in this repo.
68	
69	What was verified: the real [OpenSpec](https://github.com/Fission-AI/OpenSpec)
70	project (v1.3.x at time of writing) ships exactly this workflow,
71	installed per-repo by `openspec init --tools claude`
72	(non-interactive) as skills under `.claude/skills/openspec-*/` plus
73	`/opsx:*` command wrappers under `.claude/commands/opsx/`. The init
74	also scaffolds `openspec/` (changes/, specs/, config.yaml).
75	
76	**Choice: vendor the real library** rather than imitating its format
77	in the prompt:
78	
79	- The artifact conventions (proposal/design/tasks/specs) are
80	  maintained upstream and driven by the CLI: `openspec new change`
81	  scaffolds, `openspec instructions <artifact> --json` supplies each
82	  artifact's template and rules, and `openspec status --json` is the
83	  machine-checkable definition of "spec complete" (all
84	  `applyRequires` artifacts done).
85	- This agent runs **headless `claude -p`** (not
86	  `claude-code-action@v1`, whose PR-comment plumbing is dead weight
87	  here — same runtime-split rationale as junior-dev). The prompt
88	  therefore doesn't depend on interactive slash-command invocation:
89	  it points the agent at the vendored opsx instruction files and the
90	  `openspec` CLI, which work identically in print mode.
91	- The workflow installs the CLI on the runner
92	  (`npm install -g @fission-ai/openspec`).
93	
94	## Decision: human-in-the-loop via native PR reviews
95	
96	KAN-4 offered (A) draft PR + magic-keyword comment, or (B) Jira
97	comment + transition. **Choice: a refinement of (A) — draft PR +
98	native GitHub review verdicts** instead of magic keywords:
99	
100	- **Approve** (on the draft spec PR) → dispatches `phase=implement`.
101	- **Request changes** (body = feedback) → dispatches `phase=revise`.
102	- **Comment-only reviews and ordinary comments** → inert; they're
103	  discussion the agent reads on its next run. This is the "answer
104	  questions without triggering anything" channel.
105	- Inline review comments on specific spec lines work and reach the
106	  agent (it reads `pulls/<n>/comments` via `gh api`).
107	
108	Why this beats the alternatives:
109	
110	- vs. **magic keywords**: nothing to remember or typo; approval is
111	  the same button reviewers already use; inline-on-spec-line feedback
112	  comes for free.
113	- vs. **Jira (B)**: `pull_request_review` is a native Actions
114	  trigger, while Jira transitions would need Jira Automation →
115	  `repository_dispatch` webhook plumbing; spec diffs render properly
116	  in a PR and poorly in Jira comments.
117	
118	Safety rails in `senior-dev-resume.yml`:
119	
120	- **Draft-gated** — reviews only act while the PR is a draft. After
121	  `implement` flips it to ready, Approve/Request-changes mean
122	  ordinary code review again, and the resume workflow ignores them.
123	- **Permission-gated** — the reviewer's `author_association` must be
124	  `OWNER`/`MEMBER`/`COLLABORATOR`; drive-by reviews don't dispatch.
125	- **Acknowledged** — the workflow posts a PR comment naming the phase
126	  it dispatched, so the human knows the review landed.
127	
128	### Adaptive questions-only mode
129	
130	The propose phase decides for itself whether the ticket is specable:
131	
132	- **Confident** → full OpenSpec change + an *Assumptions* section.
133	- **Blocked** → the Draft PR carries `proposal.md` with the gathered
134	  context and numbered blocking questions only — no design/tasks
135	  built on guesses. Humans answer in comments and submit **Request
136	  changes**, which re-runs the spec phase with the answers.
137	
138	## Manual run
139	
140	```sh
141	# spec phase (what the orchestrator dispatches)
142	gh workflow run senior-dev.yml -f ticket_key=KAN-42
143	
144	# resume phases are normally review-triggered, but can be forced:
145	gh workflow run senior-dev.yml -f ticket_key=KAN-42 -f phase=implement
146	```
147	
148	Optional `max_turns` overrides `--max-turns` (phase defaults:
149	propose 30, revise 20, implement 60). Use a small value to exercise
150	the stop condition; the propose fallback opens a `[WIP]` Draft PR,
151	and resumed phases post an early-exit PR comment instead.
152	
153	## Logs
154	
155	Each run (any phase) commits its execution log to `main` at
156	`.github/agent-logs/senior-dev/<YYYY-MM-DD>/<run-id>.json` (same
157	`jq -s` array format as junior-dev) and dispatches agent-improver
158	with `agent_name=senior-dev`. Browse with `bash tools/viewer.sh`.
159	
160	## Required setup
161	
162	Same as junior-dev (see
163	[`agents/junior-dev/README.md`](../junior-dev/README.md)):
164	`CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`, Jira secrets/variables, **`DEV_PAT`**, and
165	the *Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests* repo
166	setting. Notes specific to this agent:
167	
168	- `DEV_PAT` matters doubly here: PRs/comments it authors trigger
169	  downstream workflows, and `gh pr ready` flipping the PR fires
170	  `pr-review.yml`'s `ready_for_review` trigger.
171	- `senior-dev-resume.yml` needs no extra secrets — it only uses
172	  `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`actions: write` to dispatch, `pull-requests:
173	  write` to ack). Explicit `gh workflow run` dispatches from
174	  `GITHUB_TOKEN` are allowed; the no-recursive-workflows rule only
175	  blocks *event-caused* triggers.
176	
177	## Known limitations
178	
179	- **Resume requires the workflows on `main`.** `pull_request_review`
180	  runs the workflow definition from the PR's base context, so the
181	  full loop can only be exercised after this lands on the default
182	  branch. The propose phase can be tested from any branch via
183	  `gh workflow run --ref`.
184	- **Re-review = re-dispatch.** Submitting Approve twice dispatches
185	  two implement runs; there's no dedup/lock. The ack comment makes
186	  this visible, and the second run mostly no-ops on an already-done
187	  tasks.md, but don't do it on purpose.
188	- **No automatic `/opsx:archive`.** After the implementation PR
189	  merges, archiving the change into `openspec/specs/` is a human (or
190	  future-agent) step.
191	- **No automated test enforcement** beyond what the agent chooses to
192	  run — same gap as junior-dev.
193

Based on my thorough exploration, I need to clarify what I found:

## Clarification: This is NOT a Backend/Frontend Application

The repository at `/home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness` is **my-harness**, a **reusable harness for agentic development workflows**. It is NOT a backend/frontend application with endpoints like `/style-pieces`, `/stripe`, or `/me`.

### TOP-LEVEL DIRECTORY STRUCTURE

```
/home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/
├── .claude/                      # Claude Code configuration
├── .github/workflows/            # GitHub Actions workflows
├── .jira/                        # Jira configuration (gitignored)
├── agents/                       # Agent implementations
│   ├── agent-improver/
│   ├── jira-ticket/
│   ├── junior-dev/
│   ├── pr-review/
│   └── senior-dev/
├── bin/                          # Scripts and binaries
├── docs/                         # Documentation
├── openspec/                     # OpenSpec configuration
├── scripts/                      # Installation and setup scripts
├── tools/                        # Utility tools
├── .env.example                  # Environment template
├── .gitignore
├── .mcp.json                     # MCP servers configuration
└── README.md
```

### WHAT THIS HARNESS CONTAINS

1. **Five Agentic Agents:**
   - `/agents/senior-dev/` — Spec-driven implementation for complex tickets (SP ≥ 4), with human-in-the-loop via OpenSpec
   - `/agents/junior-dev/` — Headless implementer for small tickets (SP ≤ 3)
   - `/agents/jira-ticket/` — Daily Jira backlog groomer
   - `/agents/pr-review/` — Comment-only PR reviewer
   - `/agents/agent-improver/` — Meta-agent that proposes prompt edits from execution logs

2. **GitHub Actions Workflows** (`.github/workflows/`):
   - `orchestrator.yml` — Deterministic Jira → junior/senior router on "Dev Ready" transition
   - `senior-dev.yml` & `senior-dev-resume.yml` — Multi-phase implementation with human checkpoints
   - `junior-dev.yml` — Direct implementation dispatch
   - `jira-ticket.yml` — Daily Jira grooming (cron-based)
   - `pr-review.yml` — Daily PR review
   - `agent-improver.yml` — Agent log analysis

3. **Key Integrations:**
   - Jira automation (via `repository_dispatch` webhook)
   - OpenSpec library (for spec-driven development)
   - Context7 MCP (for documentation fetching)
   - GitHub Actions orchestration

4. **Configuration Files:**
   - `.env.example` — Contains: `JIRA_API_TOKEN`, `CONTEXT7_API_KEY`
   - `.mcp.json` — Context7 MCP server configuration
   - `openspec/config.yaml` — OpenSpec project configuration

### KEY AGENT FILES

- **Senior Dev Agent:**
  - `/agents/senior-dev/prompt.md` — Three-phase conditional prompt
  - `/agents/senior-dev/README.md` — Detailed workflow documentation

- **Junior Dev Agent:**
  - `/agents/junior-dev/prompt.md`
  - `/agents/junior-dev/README.md`

- **Ticket Agent, PR Review, Agent Improver:**
  - Similar structure: `prompt.md` + `README.md`

### CRITICAL DISTINCTION

This harness is **designed to be installed into OTHER projects**. The script `/scripts/install-harness.sh` copies these files into target repositories to enable agentic development workflows there. The endpoints, models, and design systems you're asking about (`/style-pieces`, `/stripe`, `/me`, `is_subscribed` field) would exist in the TARGET project that uses this harness, not in the harness itself.

**If you're looking to explore a specific backend/frontend application that uses this harness or is a separate project, please provide the correct repository path and I'll conduct a detailed analysis of that codebase.**

Now I'm finalizing which tickets are ready to work on versus which need clarification — most of the ones I've updated should be good to go, though a few still have some ambiguity that needs resolving. I'm reconsidering which tickets actually need updates based on the principle that I should only edit when it genuinely helps. KAN-37 is missing context about why those specific tokens and colors were chosen, so I'll add that. KAN-38 is fairly self-explanatory from its title, so it might not need changes. I'm evaluating KAN-39 next to see if it has similar gaps. Brands grouped by category helps users find what they're looking for—streetwear versus old-money aesthetics. KAN-49 adds feedback and regenerate buttons to outfit cards so users can iterate without restarting. KAN-53 sets up an A/B test once Stripe and PostHog are live to measure paywall conversion improvements. For KAN-43, I'm noticing the checkout endpoint isn't named—it probably should be something like `POST /stripe/checkout-sessions`, but I need to figure out whether that's defined elsewhere or if it belongs in a separate ticket. or open questions to flag in the task description.

The checkout session endpoint is clearly essential since KAN-43 depends on it, and the standard Stripe pattern is a `POST /stripe/checkout-sessions` endpoint that returns the session URL. I'll add this directly to KAN-42's scope since all the backend Stripe work belongs there, then KAN-43 can reference that specific endpoint.

Now I need to be more systematic about which tasks need updates. Let me focus on the key changes: adding context and acceptance criteria to the epics, filling in missing context for the individual tasks, and making sure the endpoint gaps are documented. I need to execute the blocker links first since those are straightforward, then tackle the description updates. Looking back at KAN-38 and KAN-39, I realize they're mostly clear from their titles and acceptance criteria, but KAN-38 is missing context about why it establishes the base component layer before screen-level restyling—I should add that. For KAN-39, I need to check what's missing there too. I need to figure out how to link these tasks since epic link fields aren't available through the CLI—I'll use the Blocks relationship to establish the sequential dependencies instead. For KAN-35, I'm setting up KAN-37 blocking KAN-38, KAN-39, and KAN-40, with KAN-37 also directly blocking KAN-39 and KAN-40. Similarly for KAN-36, KAN-41 blocks KAN-42 and KAN-43, with KAN-41 also directly blocking KAN-43.

I'm executing all the link commands in parallel, then I'll handle the descriptions. Some descriptions will need markdown headings, so I'll write those to files first before updating the tickets. Finishing the y2k styling epic's acceptance criteria, then moving on to the subscription paywall epic which requires setting up Stripe in test mode, implementing backend webhooks to gate features behind subscription status, building the frontend paywall flow, and validating the entire end-to-end process.

Next I'm defining the design token system that underpins all the restyle work—establishing a cohesive color palette, typography scale, spacing, and border radius values through Tailwind config and CSS custom properties so the team can reference them consistently across KAN-38, KAN-39, and KAN-40 without any hard-coded values.

Then KAN-38 focuses on mechanically applying those tokens to all the form controls and buttons throughout the codebase, ensuring every interactive element inherits the new visual language while preserving existing layouts and interaction states.

KAN-39 takes the token-driven controls and applies a more editorial, finesse.us-inspired direction to the Landing and Results screens with bold typography, hero imagery, and bright brand accents.

KAN-40 brings a y2k aesthetic to the Wardrobe screen—the authenticated home users spend the most time in—using chrome accents, gradients, sticker-style badges, and serif headings, all constrained to the E1-a token palette.

KAN-41 is setting up a Stripe test account with a subscription product and recurring price, then documenting the credentials in the environment file since both the next tasks depend on this foundation.

KAN-42 is adding backend endpoints to handle Stripe checkout sessions and manage user entitlements based on subscription status.

I need to implement a checkout endpoint that creates a Stripe session, a webhook handler that updates the `is_subscribed` flag when subscriptions are created or cancelled, and middleware that enforces subscription requirements by returning a 402 status for unsubscribed users on protected routes. The acceptance criteria focus on testing these components with Stripe's test mode and CLI fixtures.

Before I proceed, I need clarification on which API endpoints should require an active subscription and whether the database schema already includes the `is_subscribed` column or if I need to create a migration for it.

For the frontend paywall implementation, I'm setting up a global HTTP 402 interceptor that redirects users to a paywall screen when they hit a gated feature without an active subscription. The paywall displays the subscription value proposition and a Subscribe button that initiates the Stripe Checkout flow. After completing checkout, the app refetches the user profile to confirm the subscription status and redirects back into the app; if the user cancels, they return to the paywall without any error messaging.

For the Results screen redesign, I'm shifting from a vertical grid to a Tinder-style horizontal swipe interface where each outfit takes up the full viewport and users can swipe between them. Within each outfit card, the products themselves are also horizontally swipeable in their own carousel, and product descriptions pulled from the database are always visible without requiring any expand interaction. This single-outfit-at-a-time focus mirrors successful discovery patterns and should drive more engagement with the buy links.

For the VibeExtraction feature, when a user enters a celebrity name and leaves the field, I'm debounce-fetching their photo and displaying it next to the extracted vibe profile to give immediate visual confirmation they typed the right name. If the photo lookup fails, the image slot just disappears cleanly without showing any error message. The acceptance criteria include getting the photo within 2 seconds for well-known celebrities, gracefully hiding the image when nothing's found, documenting the image source in code comments, and ensuring no celebrity data gets logged to analytics.

The main blocker is deciding which image API to use — Bing Image Search, SerpAPI Google Images, or Wikipedia infobox — since each has different cost, rate-limit, and reliability implications that'll shape how I implement this. That choice needs to be made before I start.

I'm also looking at the loading screen redesign where I need to replace the current multi-card swipeable loading flow with a single looping idol animation and consolidate the loading copy to just "Narcie is helping you." The animation should loop smoothly without flickering until the API response comes back, removing the complexity of the multi-stage cards.

For that animation, I still need to nail down the format — whether to use a Lottie JSON file, a short looping MP4, or a CSS-animated still image. That decision should either come from running a spike first or be decided upfront so I can move forward with implementation.

Then there's the brand picker for onboarding, where I'm restructuring the brand selection step to group brands under category headers like Streetwear, Y2K, Old Money, and Minimal. Users can select across multiple categories in one go, and those selections get stored on their profile and returned by the user API. The categories themselves should live in a static config object rather than being hardcoded, and the UI needs clear visual hierarchy to show the grouping.

For the outfit regenerate endpoint, I'm adding a POST route that takes the prior outfit and user feedback, then reinvokes the styling prompt with that context to generate a refined outfit. The endpoint needs unit tests covering feedback incorporation, constraint validation, and response schema correctness.

I'm also noting that I need clarification on what the "wearable-core rule" actually is—several tickets reference it as a constraint the regeneration must respect, but I need the specific definition to encode it properly in the prompt and write meaningful tests for it.

For KAN-49, the frontend work involves adding a feedback input and regenerate button to each outfit card, with a loading state while the request processes. On success, the card updates in-place with the new outfit; on failure, an inline error message appears with a retry option. This completes the refinement loop so users can iterate on individual outfits without restarting the whole session.

For KAN-50, I'm decomposing the styling preferences task into backend and frontend sub-tasks, with the parent description updated to reflect that split. The fields include personal color preferences and other optional styling parameters that users can specify when requesting outfits. Now I'm breaking down the implementation into two concrete tasks: one for the backend to integrate those three fields into the styling endpoint and update the Sonnet prompt, and another for the frontend to add the corresponding UI controls. Then there's a separate parent task for PostHog analytics that needs to instrument key conversion events across the app—signup with demographics, vibe extraction, outfit generation, purchase clicks, and the full paywall funnel—while ensuring we only pass hashed user IDs to avoid sending PII. KAN-52 is a post-purchase survey that triggers when users return to the app after clicking a buy link, showing a dismissible banner asking if they completed the purchase with optional feedback. The response gets submitted to the backend and stored, with the banner appearing only once per buy-link click event to avoid repetition.

I need to clarify how the app detects when a user has "returned" — whether that's through app focus events (for web or native), a deep-link return with query parameters, or a timer-based approach — since the right mechanism depends on whether we're building for web, native, or hybrid platforms.

For the A/B test paywall, I'm setting up PostHog feature flags with 2–3 variants that differ in copy and pricing, assigning them server-side when the paywall loads. I'll track `paywall_viewed`, `checkout_started`, and `subscription_completed` events per variant so we can measure conversion lift once the initial paywall and analytics are in place. The acceptance criteria include creating the flag, rendering the correct variant copy/price, sending the variant property with events, displaying results in PostHog, and falling back gracefully if the flag call times out.

Now I'm ready to execute these updates — I notice most descriptions use markdown headings, so I'll need to write them to files rather than using inline flags. The epics themselves don't need sub-tasks since their constituent tasks already exist and are linked through dependency chains — I just need to improve their descriptions without creating additional parent-child relationships. Writing the bash commands... update across 19 Jira tickets, many of which involve NHI-related changes or are decomposed parent issues.

I'm thinking through the execution strategy — I can batch the description file writes in parallel, then run the link commands together, followed by the sub-task creation for KAN-50 and KAN-51, and finally the bulk edits. Let me organize this efficiently by grouping independent operations.

Now I need to identify which tickets are actually ready to transition to Dev Ready after my updates versus which ones still need human input. Looking at the list, I've got 13 tickets that should be ready: the two epics with updated context and acceptance criteria, several tickets with added context, KAN-40 with updated AC, KAN-43 made more specific, and both KAN-50 and KAN-51 decomposed with their sub-tasks created.

The remaining six tickets are waiting on human decisions: KAN-41 needs a price point decision, KAN-42 needs clarification on which endpoints get gated, KAN-45 needs a photo API choice, KAN-46 needs an animation format decision, KAN-48 needs a wearable-core rule defined, and KAN-52 needs the return detection mechanism specified.

Actually, I'm reconsidering KAN-43 — it depends on KAN-42, but my description handles the endpoint gating through a 402 interceptor approach, so the frontend doesn't need to know which endpoints are gated upfront. That means KAN-43 could be ready independently even though KAN-42 is still blocked.

For the epics KAN-35 and KAN-36, I'm thinking about whether they should even go through the Development Ready workflow since they're containers. The epic descriptions are clear and complete after my updates, but I'm questioning whether marking epics as "Ready" makes sense in the standard Jira workflow.

For KAN-50 and KAN-51, I'm deciding whether to list the parent stories or their new sub-tasks in the Slack summary. The sub-tasks I'll create will have complete descriptions and be ready to work on immediately, but listing the parents in the summary might be cleaner for the human reviewer to understand.

The issue is that the parents are containers spanning multiple areas and shouldn't be marked Dev Ready themselves — only the atomic sub-tasks should. Since I won't know the sub-task keys until after I create them, I'll need to create them first, capture their keys, and then include those in the summary. a container for ready sub-tasks). I'll mark KAN-50 and KAN-51 as ready for dev in the summary since they're now decomposed into concrete sub-tasks. Now I'm ready to start executing—I'll draft the sub-task descriptions, beginning with the backend changes needed for KAN-50 to extend the style-pieces endpoint with the new optional fields.

When those fields are provided, I'll weave them into the Sonnet prompt to bias the outfit generation accordingly, keeping them strictly optional so existing callers aren't affected. The acceptance criteria cover API validation, a visible prompt diff in tests, and a manual spot-check that the color anchor actually influences the output. For the frontend piece, I'm adding three optional input controls to the styling form for personal color season, occasion, and color anchor before outfit generation. I need to set up PostHog event tracking for key user actions throughout the app—signup, vibe extraction, outfit generation, product clicks, paywall views, and checkout initiation—each with specific properties like age range and style goals. I'll use a hashed user ID as the distinct identifier to keep things privacy-compliant and avoid sending any PII like emails or names, then verify the events are properly captured in PostHog with the API key configured.

Now I'm moving to the backend side, installing the PostHog SDK in the same language as the backend and capturing the same events for reliability and deduplication. I'll also add server-side-only events for subscription lifecycle hooks—completion and cancellation—that fire from Stripe webhooks, maintaining the same hashed user ID approach for consistency.

For the third sub-task, I need to build a conversion funnel dashboard in PostHog once the instrumentation is live, tracking the full user journey from signup through subscription completion across six key steps.

Now I'm planning the execution strategy—I'll batch the work into four phases: first writing all the description files and setting up blocker links in parallel, then bulk-updating the issues with those descriptions, creating the sub-tasks for both epics, and finally documenting everything in a summary. I'm considering whether to run the jira CLI commands sequentially rather than in parallel to avoid rate limiting issues. I need to verify the pricing decision for KAN-41 since the monthly subscription cost hasn't been determined yet—that's blocking the Stripe Price creation. For KAN-42, I've expanded the scope to include the checkout session endpoint alongside the webhook work, which makes sense since that functionality was unassigned. Now I'm considering whether KAN-42 needs a blocker dependency.

The `STRIPE_PRICE_ID` from KAN-41 is definitely required for the checkout session, so I've already marked that dependency. On the database side, KAN-42 references setting `users.is_subscribed` to true or false, but I should clarify in the description whether the column already exists or if a migration is needed—I've captured this as an open question in the NHI section.

For KAN-53, I'm linking both KAN-43 (paywall live) and KAN-51 (PostHog live) as blockers. The mention of testing 2-3 price variants doesn't conflict with KAN-41's initial price question—that's just the control variant for the A/B test.

I'm also reconsidering whether KAN-38 should block KAN-39 and KAN-40. They do use tokens from KAN-37, and ideally the form controls would be ready before the screens are restyled, but there's no hard dependency there. The KAN-38 description mentions it's a prerequisite for screen-level work, but I'm not adding explicit Blocks links from KAN-38 to KAN-39 and KAN-40 since those are already blocked by KAN-37. I need to think through whether KAN-38 actually needs to block them or if the KAN-37 dependency is sufficient for the work to proceed in parallel. 40. So I'm adding the blocking relationships: KAN-38 blocks both KAN-39 and KAN-40, which makes sense since the form controls need to be styled first before the screen-level restyling. Let me map out all the dependencies now—I've got 11 total links connecting these tasks, which is still manageable.

But wait, I'm reconsidering whether KAN-37 should directly block KAN-39 and KAN-40 if KAN-38 already does. The chain is KAN-37 → KAN-38 → KAN-39/40, but having those direct links is actually useful because it shows all the dependencies an engineer needs to see when picking up a task. Plus, KAN-37 and KAN-38 could potentially happen in the same sprint, and KAN-39 could start once KAN-37 is done if the form controls are already in decent shape. Looking at the strict dependencies though, KAN-39 really needs the token values from KAN-37 and the button styling from KAN-38 to be complete.

Since KAN-37 blocks KAN-38, and KAN-38 blocks KAN-39, the transitivity means KAN-37 implicitly blocks KAN-39 anyway, so adding that link explicitly would be redundant. I'll stick with my original 11 links plus adding KAN-38 → KAN-39 and KAN-38 → KAN-40, which gives me 13 total, and I'll skip the redundant direct links from KAN-37 to those tasks. AN-51 blocks KAN-53, so that's 11 links total. I'm going to stop overthinking this and just execute — I'll batch the file writes and link commands together, then handle the descriptions and subtasks in follow-up messages.

But first, let me reconsider the epic updates. KAN-35's description is sparse — it mentions the y2k visual direction and references finesse.us, but lacks context and acceptance criteria even though the child tickets have the details. Same issue with KAN-36 on the subscription paywall — it's missing context and AC. Both need updating. And KAN-36 shouldn't be marked "Awaiting human input" just because KAN-41 has an open pricing question.

I've settled on moving 13 tickets to Dev Ready and leaving 6 in Awaiting human input. Time to start implementing these changes.

Apply y2k visual treatment to the Wardrobe screen (the post-login home screen): chrome/metallic accents, gradient surfaces, sticker-style item badges, and lowercase serif headings. Use E1-a token values throughout; do not introduce new colour values or change data/routing logic.

## Context
Wardrobe is the authenticated home users return to most. A distinct y2k personality here reinforces the brand's editorial identity (KAN-35) for retained users and creates a memorable "logged-in" feel distinct from the public-facing screens.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Chrome/metallic accents visible on at least the primary card and nav elements
- At least one hero or surface element uses a gradient background
- Item badges have a sticker-style treatment (rounded, outlined, or textured — consistent with the KAN-35 direction)
- Headings use lowercase serif typeface from the E1-a token set
- No hard-coded hex values; all colours sourced from E1-a tokens

input.content:
Add the following Stripe-related backend endpoints and middleware:

1. `POST /stripe/checkout-sessions` — creates a Stripe Checkout Session for the authenticated user using `STRIPE_PRICE_ID` from env, returns `{ url }`. Used by the FE (KAN-43) to redirect to Stripe.
2. `POST /stripe/webhook` — signature-verified handler. On `checkout.session.completed`, set `users.is_subscribed = true`. On `customer.subscription.deleted`, set `users.is_subscribed = false`. Reject unsigned requests with 400.
3. Entitlement middleware — rejects requests from unsubscribed users on the protected endpoints (see open question) with HTTP 402.

## Context
Provides the server-side subscription lifecycle and gating mechanism. KAN-43 (FE paywall) depends on `/stripe/checkout-sessions` and on the 402 response to trigger the paywall UI.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `/stripe/checkout-sessions` returns a valid Checkout Session URL in Stripe test mode
- `/stripe/webhook` unit-tested with Stripe CLI fixture events for `checkout.session.completed` and `customer.subscription.deleted`
- Entitlement middleware returns 402 for unsubscribed users and passes through for subscribed users on all protected routes
- Webhook signature verification rejects unsigned or tampered requests

## Needs human input
- [ ] Which endpoints should be protected by the entitlement guard? (e.g. `POST /style-pieces` only, `POST /style-pieces/regenerate` as well, or a different set?)
- [ ] Does the `users` table already have an `is_subscribed` boolean column? If not, this task must include writing and running a database migration to add it.

input.content:
Add a paywall screen and Stripe Checkout redirect flow to the frontend:

1. Global 402 interceptor — when any API call returns HTTP 402, redirect the user to the paywall screen instead of showing a generic error.
2. Paywall screen — displays subscription value prop copy and a "Subscribe" CTA. On click, calls `POST /stripe/checkout-sessions` (implemented in KAN-42) to retrieve a Checkout Session URL, then performs a full-page redirect to Stripe Checkout.
3. Return handling — on `?success=true` query param, refetch `GET /me` and redirect into the app; on `?cancelled=true`, return to the paywall screen with no error state.

## Context
Completes the subscription purchase loop. Users who hit a gated feature are caught by the 402 interceptor and routed through Checkout without bespoke per-feature gate UI.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Triggering a gated API call without a subscription redirects to the paywall screen
- Tapping Subscribe navigates to Stripe Checkout in test mode
- Completing the test checkout returns the user to the app with subscription unlocked (`GET /me` reflects `is_subscribed: true`)
- Cancelling the test checkout returns to the paywall screen with no error message

input.content:
Restructure the Results screen from a vertical grid of outfit cards to a Tinder-style horizontal swipe layout: one outfit fills the viewport at a time, the products inside each outfit are also horizontally swipeable in a carousel, and the DB-stored product description is always visible on the card without requiring any expand/tap interaction.

## Context
The grid layout requires users to scroll past products they haven't engaged with. A single-outfit swipe focuses attention per outfit and mirrors product-discovery patterns (TikTok, Tinder) that increase engagement and buy-link taps on mobile.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Swipe gesture works on touch (mobile) and mouse drag (desktop/tablet)
- Only one outfit is visible at a time; adjacent outfits may peek at the edge or be fully hidden
- Product descriptions are visible on each card without any tap-to-expand interaction
- The product carousel within each outfit card is independently horizontally swipeable
- Existing outfit data and buy-link navigation are unaffected

input.content:
After the user types a celebrity name in the vibe input and blurs the field, debounce-fetch a photo of that celebrity and render it alongside the extracted VibeProfile. If no photo is found, hide the image slot gracefully — no broken image icon, no error copy shown to the user. Document the chosen image source in a code comment or README section.

## Context
Showing the celebrity's photo immediately after name entry reinforces the vibe connection and gives users visual confirmation they typed the right name, reducing re-entry errors and making the VibeExtraction step feel more dynamic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Photo appears within 2 seconds for a commonly known celebrity (e.g. "Zendaya", "Harry Styles")
- Image slot is hidden (no broken image or error copy) when no photo is found for the entered name
- Chosen image source (API/service name) documented in a code comment or README section
- Celebrity name is not logged to server logs or sent to external analytics as PII

## Needs human input
- [ ] Which image source should be used: Bing Image Search API, SerpAPI Google Images, or Wikipedia infobox? The ticket says "pick during impl" but the choice has meaningfully different cost, rate-limit, and reliability trade-offs. Please decide before implementation starts.

input.content:
Replace the existing multi-stage swipeable loading cards with a single looping idol animation. Collapse the find-pieces and style-pieces loading phases into one copy line: "Narcie is helping you." The animation plays in a loop until the API response returns, then transitions to Results.

## Context
The current multi-card loading UX adds complexity without clear user benefit. A single animated stage is simpler, more on-brand with the idol/celebrity aesthetic, and reduces perceived wait time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Only one loading stage is shown (no swipeable multi-step cards)
- Animation loops smoothly with no flicker or visible loop seam
- Copy reads "Narcie is helping you" (or a close single-line variant approved during design review)
- Animation format decision documented in the PR description or a code comment

## Needs human input
- [ ] Which animation format should be used: Lottie JSON file, a short looping MP4, or a Claude-generated still image with CSS animation? The ticket embeds a spike to evaluate this, but the spike must happen before implementation can begin. Please either run the spike and report the outcome, or decide the format directly so implementation can start without a discovery step.

input.content:
Inside the existing onboarding flow, restructure the brand selection step so brands are displayed under category group headers (e.g. Streetwear, Y2K, Old Money, Minimal). Users can select any number of brands across categories in a single step. Selections are persisted to the user profile and returned by the user/profile API.

## Context
Grouping brands by aesthetic category helps users who don't know specific brand names discover options that match their style direction. It also enriches the profile signal available to the outfit generation engine.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Brand categories are defined as a static config object (not inline strings) so categories can be updated without touching component code
- Users can select brands across multiple categories in one interaction
- Selected brand IDs are stored on the user record and returned by the user/profile endpoint
- The UI displays brands under their category headers with clear visual grouping

input.content:
Add `POST /style-pieces/regenerate`. Request body: `{ outfit: <prior outfit object>, feedback: string }` where `feedback` is free text (e.g. "make it less formal", "swap the shoes for sneakers"). The endpoint re-invokes the Sonnet styling prompt with the prior outfit context prepended and the user's feedback incorporated, then returns a new outfit in the same schema as `POST /style-pieces`.

## Context
Gives users a single-action way to refine an outfit they mostly like, increasing satisfaction and reducing pressure to restart the full styling flow from scratch.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Endpoint accepts `outfit` object and non-empty `feedback` string; returns a new outfit matching the existing outfit response schema
- Unit tests cover: feedback text appears in the constructed prompt, wearable-core constraints are still applied, response schema is valid
- Manual spot-check: feedback "less formal" produces a measurably different outfit than the original

## Needs human input
- [ ] What is the "wearable-core rule"? Several tickets reference it as a non-negotiable invariant the regeneration must respect. Please describe the rule (e.g. "all items must be real purchasable products", "minimum of N pieces per outfit", "no costume or lingerie items") so it can be encoded in the prompt and asserted in unit tests.

On submit, call `POST /style-pieces/regenerate` (KAN-48) with the current outfit object and the feedback text. Show a card-level loading state while the request is in-flight. Completes the outfit refinement loop started by KAN-48. Users can iterate on individual outfits without restarting the full styling session, improving perceived quality and reducing drop-off.

- Card shows a loading state (spinner or skeleton) while the regenerate request is in-flight
- Successful regeneration replaces the card content in-place without navigating away

input.content:
Add optional styling preference fields to the outfit generation flow. Decomposed into two sub-tasks: backend API extension (BE sub-task) and frontend controls (FE sub-task).

Fields:
- `personal_color_season` — enum: spring | summer | autumn | winter
- `occasion` — free text or preset enum (e.g. "date night", "office casual")
- `color_anchor` — hex value or colour name (e.g. "#FF4D4D", "cobalt blue")

When provided, each field is woven into the Sonnet styling prompt to bias outfit output accordingly. All fields are strictly optional.

## Context
Allows users to give more directed style input beyond the celebrity vibe, producing more personally relevant outfits and giving the team additional profile signal for future personalisation work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All three fields optional; omitting them produces the same outfit quality as before
- When `color_anchor` is set, sample outfits demonstrably include or lean toward that colour
- Prompt diff (with vs. without fields) visible in the PR
- FE controls collect and submit all three fields, each with a clear label and appropriate input type

input.content:
Instrument the app with PostHog analytics to capture key conversion events across frontend and backend. Decomposed into three sub-tasks: FE SDK and event tracking, BE SDK and server-side event tracking, and funnel dashboard.

Events to capture: `signup` (with self-reported demographics), `vibe_extracted`, `outfit_generated`, `buy_link_clicked`, and paywall funnel steps (`paywall_viewed`, `checkout_started`, `subscription_completed`).

PII rule: pass hashed user IDs only (`sha256(user_id)`); never send email, display name, or raw user ID to PostHog.

## Context
Without analytics the team is flying blind on the conversion funnel. PostHog data will directly inform A/B test decisions (KAN-53) and sprint prioritisation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All listed events fire in PostHog under the expected event names
- No PII (email, name, raw user ID) appears in any PostHog event property
- A funnel dashboard exists in PostHog showing the steps from `signup` to `subscription_completed`
- README updated with event name glossary and PII policy summary

input.content:
After a user taps a product buy link, detect when they next return to the app and show a one-time dismissible banner: "Did you buy [product name]?" with Yes / No buttons and an optional free-text reason field. Submit the response to `POST /survey/purchase-intent` and persist it. The banner is shown at most once per buy-link click event and does not re-appear after dismissal or submission.

## Context
Measures real purchase conversion — a metric the team cannot infer from buy-link clicks alone. Responses seed the dataset for future recommendation quality improvements and investor reporting.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Banner shown at most once per buy-link click event (not once per session or per product)
- User can dismiss the banner without answering; no response is persisted on dismissal
- Yes/No + optional reason submitted to backend and persisted in the database
- Banner does not reappear for the same buy-link click after either dismissal or submission

## Needs human input
- [ ] How should the app detect that the user has "returned" after clicking a buy link? Options: (a) `visibilitychange` / app-regains-focus event fires on the same tab/webview, (b) a deep-link return URL routes back to the app with a query param identifying the click event, (c) a short timer fires after the external link opens and shows the banner on re-focus. The correct mechanism depends on whether the app is a web SPA, a native wrapper, or a PWA — please clarify.

input.content:
Using PostHog feature flags, set up a 2–3 variant A/B test over the paywall screen. Each variant differs in headline copy and/or displayed price. Variants are assigned client-side via a PostHog flag evaluated on paywall load. Track `paywall_viewed`, `checkout_started`, and `subscription_completed` events with a `variant` property on each.

Depends on: Stripe paywall live (KAN-43), PostHog instrumentation live (KAN-51).

## Context
Provides data-driven optimisation of paywall conversion after the initial paywall and analytics are live. Even a small lift in conversion rate compounds significantly with user acquisition scale.

## Acceptance Criteria
- PostHog feature flag created with 2–3 named variants (e.g. `control`, `price-anchor`, `social-proof`)
- Paywall screen reads the active variant from PostHog and renders the corresponding copy and/or price display
- `paywall_viewed` event sent with `{ variant }` property on every paywall load
- PostHog experiment or funnel dashboard shows conversion rate per variant
- If the PostHog flag call fails or times out, the screen falls back to the default variant without error

Acceptance Criteria:

- No required field changes — existing callers without the new fields continue to work unchanged" --no-input 2>&1

- checkout_started — fired when the user taps Subscribe and the redirect begins

Acceptance Criteria:
- All six events appear in the PostHog project under the correct event names
- No email, name, or raw user ID in any PostHog event property
- POSTHOG_API_KEY added to frontend env and .env.example" --no-input 2>&1

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Create sub-tasks under KAN-50 and KAN-51 via Jira REST API."""
import urllib.request
import urllib.error
import json
import os
import base64
import sys

server = "https://jaeyoonsworkspace-23358853.atlassian.net"
login = "jaeyoonbae99@gmail.com"
token = os.environ["JIRA_API_TOKEN"]
creds = base64.b64encode(f"{login}:{token}".encode()).decode()
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Basic {creds}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}

def get_issue_type_id(name):
    url = f"{server}/rest/api/3/issuetype"
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers=headers)
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as r:
        types = json.loads(r.read())
    for t in types:
        if t["name"].lower() == name.lower():
            return t["id"]
    return None

def get_project_id(key):
    url = f"{server}/rest/api/3/project/{key}"
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers=headers)
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as r:
        return json.loads(r.read())["id"]

def create_subtask(parent_key, summary, description):
    subtask_id = get_issue_type_id("Subtask") or get_issue_type_id("Sub-task") or get_issue_type_id("Sub-Task")
    if not subtask_id:
        print("ERROR: Could not find Sub-task issue type", file=sys.stderr)
        return None

    project_id = get_project_id("KAN")

    payload = {
        "fields": {
            "project": {"id": project_id},
            "parent": {"key": parent_key},
            "summary": summary,
            "description": {
                "type": "doc",
                "version": 1,
                "content": [
                    {
                        "type": "paragraph",
                        "content": [{"type": "text", "text": description}]
                    }
                ]
            },
            "issuetype": {"id": subtask_id},
        }
    }

    url = f"{server}/rest/api/3/issue"
    data = json.dumps(payload).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, data=data, headers=headers, method="POST")
    try:
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as r:
            result = json.loads(r.read())
            print(f"Created: {result['key']} - {summary}")
            return result['key']
    except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
        body = e.read().decode()
        print(f"ERROR creating sub-task: {e.code} {body}", file=sys.stderr)
        return None


subtasks = [
    # KAN-50 sub-tasks
    (
        "KAN-50",
        "BE: Add personal_color_season, occasion, color_anchor to /style-pieces and Sonnet prompt",
        "Extend the POST /style-pieces request body with three optional fields: personal_color_season (enum: spring | summer | autumn | winter), occasion (string, max 100 chars, e.g. 'date night'), and color_anchor (string — hex or colour name, e.g. '#FF4D4D'). When any field is provided, weave it into the Sonnet prompt to bias the outfit output accordingly. All fields are strictly optional; omitting them must not degrade outfit quality.\n\nAcceptance Criteria:\n- API accepts and validates the three optional fields; rejects unknown values for personal_color_season with a 422\n- Prompt diff (with fields set vs. not set) visible in unit test or PR description\n- When color_anchor is set, manual spot-check confirms the returned outfit skews toward that colour\n- No required field changes — existing callers without the new fields continue to work unchanged",
    ),
    (
        "KAN-50",
        "FE: Add personal color, occasion, and color anchor controls to styling flow",
        "Add three optional input controls to the styling form before the user submits for outfit generation: (1) Personal colour season — segmented control or dropdown with Spring / Summer / Autumn / Winter options. (2) Occasion — text input with optional preset suggestions (e.g. 'Date night', 'Office', 'Casual'). (3) Colour anchor — colour name text input or colour picker. Submit these fields in the /style-pieces request body when non-empty.\n\nAcceptance Criteria:\n- All three controls are clearly labelled and optional; users can submit without filling them\n- Non-empty values are included in the API request body\n- Controls are keyboard-navigable with visible labels (accessible)\n- Existing users who skip all controls get the same outfit experience as before",
    ),
    # KAN-51 sub-tasks
    (
        "KAN-51",
        "FE: PostHog SDK setup and frontend event tracking",
        "Install PostHog JS SDK in the frontend. Capture: signup (properties: age_range, style_goals — no PII), vibe_extracted, outfit_generated, buy_link_clicked (properties: product_id, outfit_index), paywall_viewed, checkout_started. PII rule: pass sha256(user_id) as the PostHog distinct ID; never send email or display name.\n\nAcceptance Criteria:\n- All six events appear in the PostHog project under the correct event names\n- No email, name, or raw user ID in any PostHog event property\n- POSTHOG_API_KEY added to frontend env and .env.example",
    ),
    (
        "KAN-51",
        "BE: PostHog SDK setup and server-side event tracking",
        "Install PostHog SDK (match backend language). Capture the same event set as the FE sub-task from the server side, plus subscription_completed (on checkout.session.completed webhook) and subscription_cancelled (on customer.subscription.deleted webhook). PII rule: hashed user ID as distinct ID only.\n\nAcceptance Criteria:\n- Server-side events appear in PostHog for the relevant flows\n- subscription_completed and subscription_cancelled events visible after Stripe webhook fixture tests\n- No PII in PostHog event properties\n- POSTHOG_API_KEY added to backend env and backend/.env.example",
    ),
    (
        "KAN-51",
        "PostHog: Build conversion funnel dashboard",
        "In the PostHog project, create a funnel insight from signup -> vibe_extracted -> outfit_generated -> paywall_viewed -> checkout_started -> subscription_completed. Save it as a dashboard named 'Conversion Funnel'. Requires FE and BE event instrumentation (sub-tasks 1 and 2) to be deployed first.\n\nAcceptance Criteria:\n- 'Conversion Funnel' dashboard exists in PostHog with all six funnel steps\n- Funnel shows conversion rates between each step\n- README updated with the dashboard name and how to locate it in PostHog",
    ),
]

for parent, summary, description in subtasks:
    create_subtask(parent, summary, description)

Excellent! Now I have important information from the raw JSON of KAN-37:

1. Issue type IDs:
   - Task: 10004
   - Epic: 10001

2. Project ID: 10000 (KAN project)

Also interesting: the ticket already has a well-formed description with a `Needs human input` section from a previous run! Looking at the description, KAN-37 already has:
- A reference to `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css` (the `.narcie-root` class)
- Reference to `frontend/src/App.jsx` 
- A "Needs human input" section about Storybook

This is concerning—it looks like my description update overwrote content that was already carefully groomed by a previous agent run with actual file paths from the codebase.

Looking at the raw JSON output more carefully, the timestamp shows the description was just updated, and I can see the content object contains references to `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css` and what appears to be acceptance criteria that got cut off. I need to check whether my generic description actually replaced the more detailed, file-specific content that was there before. I'm noticing a discrepancy between what I wrote in my description and what's showing up in the Jira JSON—there are specific file paths like `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css` that weren't in my original description. This suggests either a previous agent run already added those details and I overwrote them, or my update somehow didn't take. But the confirmation said the issue was updated successfully, so I need to figure out what's actually in Jira right now versus what I intended to write. The jira-cli might be reporting success while the actual update failed or didn't take effect, or the content was already there before I tried to update it. Let me verify by checking the current description directly to see if my changes actually persisted. The description in the raw JSON output is completely different from what I wrote to the file — it mentions specific file paths and CSS custom properties instead of Tailwind config. This suggests a previous agent run already updated KAN-37 with actual implementation details from the codebase, so my piped edit either didn't take effect or got overwritten. I'm realizing the timing might be the issue here—the raw JSON shows an update timestamp that could match when I made my changes, but the content itself doesn't reflect what I actually wrote. Let me trace through the exact sequence of when things were updated versus when I viewed them to figure out if I'm looking at my own changes or something else entirely. The description files were definitely written before the edit commands ran since they were in the first batch, but when I check the raw output from viewing KAN-37, it contains file paths like `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css` that couldn't have come from my description file or the original one. I'm realizing a previous agent run today must have already updated KAN-37 with those code-grounded details. The specific detail about CSS custom properties without Tailwind suggests a previous agent had actual access to the codebase and wrote an accurate description, which I may have just overwritten with my generic version. The raw view is showing that better description, not my edit.

Looking back at the jira CLI documentation, I realize the issue: the `-b` flag is what sets the body/description for the edit command, but when piping stdin with `--template -`, it might only work for the create command. My updates to all 19 tickets probably returned "OK" but didn't actually change the descriptions because the piping didn't properly map to the body field.

I can verify this by checking the timestamp on one of the edited issues — if it's recent (today, 2026-06-20), the edit went through, but the content might look different because the jira CLI converts markdown to ADF format, which could explain why my description doesn't match what I see in the raw JSON output.

Wait, but the descriptions are completely different — mine talks about defining a new token set and wiring it into Tailwind or CSS custom properties, while the one in the JSON is about updating an existing tokens file with a brighter palette and no Tailwind. These aren't just formatting differences, they're entirely different tasks.

The "✓ Issue updated" messages suggest the API calls went through, but maybe they only updated metadata like timestamps, not the actual description body. Or the Jira CLI converted my markdown to ADF in a way that didn't actually persist the changes I intended.

Looking at what I sent versus what's in the raw JSON view, there's a clear mismatch — my markdown file describes a Tailwind config approach, but the ADF shows "No Tailwind" and references a CSS file directly. This suggests my edits either didn't take effect or got overwritten by something else. means the API call succeeded, or the raw JSON view might be cached from before my update. The real issue is that the raw output shows a previous high-quality description mentioning actual file paths like `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css`, which means either my update didn't work or I'm looking at stale data. I should check the plain view of KAN-37 to see what the current description actually is after my supposed edit.

Looking more carefully at the raw JSON, the description field has ADF structure with multiple sections including "Acceptance Criteria" and "Needs human input", whereas the plain view I ran earlier only showed simple text. It's possible the plain view is truncating to just the first paragraph while the raw view shows the full structured content. The `--plain` flag probably only shows a summary while `--raw` displays the full ADF content, which would explain why I'm seeing different descriptions. My edit seems to have updated the ticket, but the raw view is showing content that doesn't match what I wrote—so let me check the current state of KAN-37 in plain mode to see what's actually there now.

content.text: This raw output reveals something important: KAN-37 already has a rich, code-grounded description from a previous agent run (with real file paths like `frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css`). My pipe updates may have overwritten better content. Let me verify the current state.

input.command: jira issue view KAN-37 --plain 2>&1

content.content:
⭐ Task  🚧 Idea  ⌛ Sat, 20 Jun 26  👷 Unassigned  🔑️ KAN-37  💭 0 comments  🧵 3 linked                          
                                                                                                                      
  # Design tokens: colors, type scale, spacing, radii                                                                 
                                                                                                                      
  ⏱️  Fri, 19 Jun 26  🔎 Jae Yoon Bae  🚀 Medium  📦 None  🏷️  design, sprint-1  👀 You are watching                  
                                                                                                                      
  ------------------------ Description ------------------------                                                       
                                                                                                                      
  Update the token set in frontend/src/styles/narcie-tokens.css (the .narcie-root class already encapsulates the      
  current                                                                                                             
  tokens) to reflect the new brighter Gen-Z palette: revised colour ramp, type scale, spacing scale, and radii. No    
  Tailwind — the project uses pure CSS custom properties.                                                             
                                                                                                                      
  ## Acceptance Criteria                                                                                              
                                                                                                                      
  • narcie-tokens.css contains a documented colour ramp, type scale, spacing scale, and radius set matching the new   
  Gen-Z/editorial direction                                                                                           
  • The .narcie-root wrapper class is applied at the root level in frontend/src/App.jsx (or frontend/src/main.jsx ) so
  all screens inherit the tokens                                                                                      
  • No hard-coded hex values in the token definitions themselves (values must be the final resolved colours)

Wait actually, let me think again. The `--plain` view NOW (after my supposed update) still shows the previous agent's content. So my updates didn't take effect. Let me verify this by looking at the format.

Actually — looking at KAN-50: "Add three optional styling preference fields to POST /style-pieces" with specific details like `style-pieces.dto.ts` and `StylePiecesDto`. 

So my `jira issue edit` commands returned "OK" but didn't actually update anything. The pipe approach isn't working, which means the descriptions I set are still the previous agent's code-grounded ones. Those are actually better than what I wrote since they reference actual file paths and implementation details.

Looking at the tickets now, I see the previous agent already did most of the grooming work. KAN-37 has specific file references and a "Needs human input" section about Storybook, and KAN-50 has the DTO file paths with a question about UI control placement. Both are well-structured except for those open questions. Since my updates failed, I should reassess all the tickets to see what still needs attention.
